archaeology history books Fragments of the Iliad by Homer have been found buried with an Egyptian mummy. The papyrus contained a section from Book II called the 'Catalogue of Ships' which describes the Greeks preparing for invasion and the forces that were in front of Troy. The mummy which died sometime between the 1st and 2nd CE was found along with several others. Out and About – Frosty Items Here are some black and white images of frost and ice crystals taken on two different days last year. In November, we walked around a part of Fornebu, just outside of Oslo, and enjoyed the crystals growing on various items — and in December, I shot a bit of frosty nature around our garden. I always love when ice crystals grow on those frosty, humid mornings. It’s the “good” kind of ice. A frosty lock on a brygge A bit of frost on the wood denoting a parking spot The brygge itself was frosty (and slippery!) More wood with ice crystals-a-growing Ice crystals growing even on some random plastic jug left about A frosty spiky seed pod Little islands of baby crystals trying to grow on the lid of our compost bin Four-leaf and frosty Cold, curled leaves Frost on our thujas too Long, long grass at Fornebu Shorter, thick grass in our garden Fancy crystals growing on the frozen surface of a water dish in the garden Abstract AbstractPhotography BlackAndWhitePhotography Frost Frozen Ice IceCrystals Norway Photoblog Photography winter

Fast‑tracked online degree programs are allowing some students to earn a bachelor’s degree in just weeks instead of the traditional four‑year timeline, prompting worry among educators about the quality and legitimacy of such accelerated credentials. Critics argue that compressing coursework may undermine rigorous learning standards, while proponents claim the flexibility meets the demands of today’s fast‑moving job market. The debate highlights broader concerns over the rise of for‑profit online colleges and the need for clearer oversight to protect students and maintain educational integrity. You're heading home to your grandfather's funeral, who had been missing for several years, when you get into an accident after driving in torrential rain. Waking up, you find yourself in a place that looks familiar to your childhood, yet has a dream like quality that doesn't exist in reality. Assisting a young girl you encounter, you journey towards the peak of the local mountain, while interacting with a cast of animal like characters who inhabit this new world. Armed with your trusty camera, can you learn and really see what's happening to both the inhabitants of this place, and you? A narrative adventure, this game explores memory, childhood, growing up and how relationships shape and forge connections across your lifetime. Using a camera, you'll take photos of everything in order to piece together the events that currently plague the inhabitants of this land, and find the Seer who seems to be the core of the issues, tracing their journey through a land that looks uncomfortably familiar. I quite enjoyed this game, the initial UX for save points and checkpoints were a little awkward if you're trying to practically 100% the run, getting all the appropriate photos for the 'good' ending. They did improve the save system about halfway through my playthrough, so you could easily reload past sections so you weren't locked out of areas, but some transitions were still poorly signposted, which made it very easy to progress the story when you were not expecting to. A good game to take your time in, I think I spent 12hrs or so, seeing all there was to see, poking around in every corner in an attempt to get all the endgame content unlocked. A beautiful game in scenery and style. I played it with Chinese audio and English text, which I felt was the best combo. The cast of characters were immensely charismatic and enjoyable, with unique personalities and fabulous design, I loved all of them and looked forward to every interaction, my favourite being the train conductor. A recommended game, if you want a chill exploratory game you don't have to white knuckle (although a couple of instances were by the seat of my pants)
